text: Ventromesh Rectopaxi has gained popularity in the treatment of full thickness rectal prolapse in view of reported lower recurrence rates and lesser post-operative constipation.

This is a forty-five-year Chinese female with significant obstetric history of four normal vaginal deliveries with episiotomies. She presented with a history of obstructed defecation and a Wexner constipation score of nineteen.
The examination reveals no visible rectal prolapse, an anterior rectocele, and normal anal tone.
The defecating proctogram image reveals an abnormal descent of the anal rectal junction, visible intra-rectal interception, and an anterior rectocele.
The patient is positioned in a modified Lloyd-Davies position, with a bladder catheter inserted. The video shows the placement of laparoscopic ports on the abdominal surface.
The surgical setup includes a 10mm subarm ligoport and two 5mm ports positioned on each side of the patient's abdomen.
The surgeon retracts the uterus to expose the pelvic area, revealing the site of intrarectal interception. Transabdominal proline sutures are placed to secure the bulky uterus.
The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision over the sacral promontory, extending it distally in an inverted J shape along the right side of the rectum and over the deepest part of the pouch of Douglas.
The surgeon performs an anterior-only rectal mobilization, carefully manipulating the rectum to avoid damage to the lateral neurovascular pedicles.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection limited to the sacral promontory, focusing on the area without extending further into the surrounding tissues.
A malleable retractor is inserted into the vagina to delineate the boundaries of the vaginal wall, aiding in the visualization and facilitation of the dissection process.

The surgeon incises the rectal-vaginal septum and dissects it down to the pelvic floor, reaching approximately two to three centimeters above the dentate line, past the level of the levator ani muscle.
This can also be confirmed on digital rectal examination.
The surgeon trims a proline mesh into an L-shaped configuration and secures it onto the pelvic floor muscles on either side of the upper inner canal using tackers.

The surgeon performs rectopexy and levetopexy by securing the mesh to the pelvic floor muscles on either side of the upper anal canal, ensuring that the tackers are not placed directly into the rectal wall.
The surgeon positions the mesh without suturing or stapling it directly to the rectal wall, avoiding potential mesh infection and erosion.
The surgeon anchors the L-shaped proline mesh to the sacral promontory using Etibon II-O sutures, securing it in place as part of the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum over the mesh using V-lock sutures, concealing the mesh beneath the peritoneal layer.
The final surgical result shows the mesh securely positioned over the pelvic floor muscles, avoiding direct contact with the rectal wall, as part of the rectopexy and levetopexy procedure.
The total laparoscopic time was a hundred and twenty minutes. The patient went home well on post-op day one.

The Association of Colour Proctology of Great Britain and Ireland recommends against the use of prosthetic meshes because of its risk of erosion and fissilation. A recent systemic review found increased mesh erosion rates with synthetic meshes as compared to biologic meshes. During our initial experience, proline meshes were used instead of biologic meshes as they were not available.
However, in the past one year, we have replaced proline meshes with biologic meshes as they have been made available. Suture rectopexy is not preferred due to poor reported outcomes.
Thank you for your kind attention.
